table_of_contents.title: Autoregressive NLG models and teacher forcing
Why maximum-probability decoding fails for open-ended text
Exposure bias and training beyond MLE

units.name: What NLG is and how tasks vary by open-endedness
units.description: Defines natural language generation, surveys representative applications, and introduces the open-ended versus non-open-ended task spectrum.
units.summary: The segment defines natural language generation as NLP where the output is natural language, contrasting it with understanding tasks whose input is language [ts=155s]. It notes a historical shift from rule-based templates and infilling to deep learning systems [ts=189s]. The lecture then surveys examples including machine translation, digital assistants, summarization, creative story writing, data-to-text, image captioning, and ChatGPT as a general-purpose NLG system [ts=214s] [ts=280s]. It organizes tasks by open-endedness: translation and summarization have relatively constrained output spaces, dialogue allows multiple valid continuations, and story generation has extremely large valid output space [ts=348s] [ts=432s]. The segment closes by formalizing this spectrum with entropy, arguing that high-entropy tasks are more open-ended and that this distinction will matter for both decoding and training choices [ts=493s].

key_points.text: Natural language generation focuses on systems whose outputs are fluent, coherent, and useful natural language.
Representative NLG tasks include machine translation, dialogue, summarization, story generation, data-to-text, and image captioning.
Open-ended generation has a high degree of freedom in valid outputs, while non-open-ended generation is largely determined by the input.
Task open-endedness can be formalized with entropy: higher entropy implies more open-ended generation.

units.name: Autoregressive generation, architectures, and teacher forcing
units.description: Reviews left-to-right autoregressive language modeling, architecture choices, and the standard maximum-likelihood training setup.
units.summary: This segment reviews autoregressive language modeling: at each step the model scores the vocabulary, applies softmax to obtain the next-token distribution, chooses a token, and feeds that prediction back in recursively until the sequence ends [ts=528s]. It then connects task type to architecture, describing encoder-decoder systems as the usual choice for less open-ended tasks like machine translation and decoder-only autoregressive models as the common choice for more open-ended generation, while emphasizing that this is a practical convention rather than a hard constraint [ts=577s] [ts=638s]. The lecture revisits maximum-likelihood training as next-token prediction under teacher forcing, where gold previous tokens are provided during training [ts=672s]. It distinguishes this from generation-time student forcing, where the model must condition on its own earlier outputs, and clarifies that “autoregressive” refers to generating left-to-right by chaining over previously generated tokens [ts=729s] [ts=742s].

key_points.text: Autoregressive generation predicts one token at a time from a softmax next-token distribution and feeds predictions back into the model.
Encoder-decoder models are commonly preferred for less open-ended tasks such as machine translation, while decoder-only models are common for more open-ended generation.
Teacher forcing trains by maximizing the probability of the next token given gold previous tokens.
Student forcing uses the model's own generated text as context at generation time.
Autoregressive means generating left to right by conditioning on tokens just generated via the chain rule.

units.name: Greedy and beam decoding under repetition pressure
units.description: Explains why maximizing model probability works for constrained tasks but degenerates for open-ended generation.
units.summary: The segment defines decoding as the function that chooses a token from the model's next-token distribution [ts=915s]. It reviews greedy decoding and beam search as maximum-probability methods aimed at finding the most likely string, which is sensible for low-entropy tasks such as translation and summarization [ts=934s] [ts=968s]. The lecture then shows why this objective fails for open-ended generation: the most likely continuations become repetitive, and plots reveal a self-amplification effect in which repeated phrases receive increasingly favorable probability or lower negative log-likelihood as repetition continues [ts=1008s] [ts=1039s]. This degeneration appears in both LSTMs and transformers and does not disappear just by scaling to larger models [ts=1070s]. N-gram blocking is introduced as a simple heuristic, but it can wrongly ban legitimate reuse such as names appearing multiple times [ts=1098s]. The broader takeaway is that maximum-probability decoding does not match human uncertainty patterns, since human text shows fluctuating confidence while beam-decoded text is unrealistically overconfident [ts=1232s].

key_points.text: Greedy decoding chooses the highest-probability token at each time step, while beam search keeps multiple candidate continuations.
Maximum-probability decoding is suitable for low-entropy tasks but encounters problems on open-ended generation.
Repeated phrases can become more probable as they repeat, revealing a self-amplification repetition effect.
N-gram blocking prevents exact repeated n-grams but can incorrectly ban valid reuse such as repeated names.
Human token probabilities fluctuate more than beam-decoded text, suggesting argmax-style decoding mismatches human generation patterns.

units.name: Sampling-based decoding, truncation, temperature, and reranking
units.description: Builds the main stochastic decoding toolbox for open-ended generation and explains how reranking can improve sampled outputs.
units.summary: This segment argues that if maximum-probability decoding is wrong for open-ended text, decoding should introduce randomness by sampling from the next-token distribution [ts=1356s]. Vanilla sampling increases diversity but suffers from heavy-tailed vocabularies, where many individually low-probability bad tokens still have substantial aggregate mass [ts=1386s]. To cut the tail, top-k sampling restricts choices to the k highest-probability tokens, but the lecture shows fixed k can either remove valid options or leave implausible ones, creating recall and precision problems depending on the shape of the distribution [ts=1456s] [ts=1671s]. Nucleus or top-p sampling addresses this by sampling from the smallest candidate set whose cumulative probability exceeds p, giving an adaptive effective k [ts=1741s] [ts=1769s]. The segment also sketches typical sampling and epsilon sampling as other truncation families [ts=1875s]. Temperature scaling is presented as an orthogonal control: larger temperature flattens the distribution and increases diversity, while smaller temperature sharpens it and approaches greedy decoding [ts=2009s]. Finally, reranking is introduced: sample multiple candidates, score them with perplexity or richer objectives such as style, coherence, entailment, factuality, and consistency, and choose the best candidate; multiple rerankers can be composed [ts=2150s] [ts=2218s].

key_points.text: Sampling introduces randomness so the model no longer always follows the single highest-probability continuation.
Heavy-tailed token distributions make vanilla sampling risky because bad low-probability words have substantial aggregate probability mass.
Top-k sampling truncates the vocabulary to the k highest-probability tokens.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling uses cumulative probability mass, giving an adaptive effective k across different distributions.
Typical sampling and epsilon sampling are alternative truncation strategies based on entropy or minimum probability thresholds.
Temperature rescales logits: tau greater than 1 flattens the distribution, tau less than 1 sharpens it, and tau near 0 approximates greedy decoding.
Reranking samples multiple candidates and selects the one with the best quality score, potentially combining style and factuality objectives.

units.name: Exposure bias, alternative training schemes, and RLHF context
units.description: Connects training-time mismatch to generation errors and surveys methods that go beyond plain MLE.
units.summary: The lecture revisits repetition from the training side and argues that maximum-likelihood language models can assign high probability to bad degenerate strings [ts=2473s]. The core diagnosis is exposure bias: under teacher forcing the model trains on gold prefixes, but at generation time it must condition on its own imperfect outputs, creating a harmful train-test mismatch [ts=2510s] [ts=2541s]. Scheduled sampling narrows this gap by mixing model-generated and gold tokens with a probability that increases through training, though the objective can be unstable [ts=2560s]. DAgger similarly augments training data with model-generated sequences so the model learns on states it will actually encounter [ts=2603s]. The lecture also presents retrieval-augmented generation, where the model begins from a retrieved prototype and edits it by insertion, deletion, or swapping, reducing exposure bias because generation no longer starts from scratch left-to-right [ts=2655s]. Reinforcement learning is then framed as an MDP over states, actions, policies, and rewards, with rewards derived from automatic metrics or human preference [ts=2693s] [ts=2827s]. The lecture warns that optimizing BLEU or ROUGE can game the metric rather than improve true quality [ts=2744s]. It finishes with the modern pipeline behind ChatGPT-style systems: large-scale pretraining, instruction tuning, and then RLHF, with RLHF viewed as difficult from scratch but effective on top of a strong pretrained model [ts=3110s] [ts=3130s].

key_points.text: MLE-trained language models can place high probability on degenerate repetitive strings.
Exposure bias is the mismatch between training on gold prefixes and generating from model-produced prefixes at test time.
Scheduled sampling mixes generated and gold tokens during training, gradually increasing the amount of model-generated context.
DAgger reduces train-test mismatch by adding model-generated sequences into the training distribution.
Retrieval-augmented generation starts from a retrieved prototype and edits it, reducing exposure bias.
RL for NLG treats next-token selection as actions with rewards from metrics or human preference, but optimizing proxy rewards can game the objective.
A modern ChatGPT-style pipeline is pretraining, then instruction tuning, then RLHF.

units.name: Automatic evaluation metrics for NLG
units.description: Introduces lexical-overlap and embedding-based automatic metrics, along with their main strengths and failure modes.
units.summary: The segment begins the evaluation portion by separating methods into content-overlap metrics, model-based metrics, and human evaluations [ts=3310s]. It explains that lexical-overlap metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE are cheap, fast, and widely used, especially in machine translation and summarization [ts=3323s]. Their weakness is that they operate on word overlap: they can miss semantically equivalent paraphrases and can also reward outputs with high lexical overlap but wrong meaning, yielding both false negatives and false positives [ts=3349s] [ts=3460s]. The lecture emphasizes that these metrics degrade as tasks become more open-ended, from summarization to dialogue to story generation [ts=3387s]. It then transitions to model-based metrics that compare texts through learned embeddings rather than raw n-grams [ts=3515s]. Examples include vector similarity, word mover's distance using optimal transport, BERTScore using contextual embeddings and alignment, sentence-embedding similarity, and BLEURT as a BERT-based regression model for text quality relative to a reference [ts=3582s] [ts=3627s] [ts=3678s].

key_points.text: Automatic evaluation splits into content-overlap metrics, model-based metrics, and human evaluations.
BLEU and ROUGE are cheap and fast but rely on lexical overlap with a reference.
Lexical-overlap metrics suffer both false negatives for paraphrases and false positives for wrong answers with overlapping words.
Content-overlap metrics become progressively worse as NLG tasks become more open-ended.
Model-based metrics use learned embeddings to measure semantic similarity rather than raw n-gram overlap.
Word mover's distance aligns source and target embeddings with optimal transport.
BERTScore and BLEURT are embedding-based metrics that use contextual alignment or BERT regression to score generated text.

units.name: Evaluating open-ended generation and the role of human judgment
units.description: Covers MAUVE, metric-to-human correlation, and the practical limits of human evaluation.
units.summary: This segment asks how to evaluate genuinely open-ended generation, where strict semantic similarity to one reference can be misleading [ts=3718s]. It introduces MAUVE as a distributional metric that computes information divergence in a quantized embedding space between generated text and human-written reference text, motivated by the need to compare batches of outputs rather than exact sentence matches [ts=3738s] [ts=3777s]. The lecture argues MAUVE is more applicable to open-ended settings because it captures a precision-recall view over the target distribution rather than direct reference overlap [ts=3914s]. It then steps back to ask how to evaluate evaluation metrics themselves, proposing correlation with human judgment as the gold criterion and using weak BLEU-human correlation as evidence that BLEU is not a very good general metric [ts=4048s]. Human evaluation is presented as the gold standard for model assessment and for developing automatic metrics, with annotators asked to judge qualities like fluency, coherence, factuality, style, and common sense [ts=4115s]. But human studies are slow, expensive, inconsistent, difficult to calibrate across papers, and fundamentally precision-only rather than recall-aware [ts=4153s] [ts=4226s]. The segment closes by mentioning hybrid approaches that learn metrics from human judgments, divide labor between humans and models, or evaluate interactive co-authoring experience [ts=4227s].

key_points.text: For open-ended generation, evaluating exact semantic similarity to a single reference can be the wrong objective.
MAUVE measures information divergence between generated and reference text distributions in a quantized embedding space.
MAUVE is motivated by open-ended settings where outputs can be high quality without closely matching a reference story.
A good automatic metric should correlate strongly with human judgment; weak BLEU-human correlation is evidence against BLEU as a general metric.
Human evaluation typically scores fluency, coherence, factuality, style, and common sense.
Human evaluation is slow, expensive, inconsistent, not well calibrated across studies, and measures precision rather than recall.
Hybrid evaluation can learn from human judgments or combine human precision assessment with model-based recall assessment.

units.name: Ethical risks, deployment safeguards, and open research problems
units.description: Surveys safety, bias, truthfulness, and adversarial robustness concerns for deployed NLG systems.
units.summary: The ethics section argues that as language models improve, alignment with human values becomes more urgent [ts=4332s]. It stresses that models should avoid harmful and toxic content and should be unbiased and fair across demographic groups [ts=4341s]. The lecture uses ChatGPT prompt refusal as a positive example but notes that jailbreak prompting can bypass safeguards and elicit harmful output [ts=4357s] [ts=4371s]. It also warns that large language models may be convincing while factually wrong, so truthfulness is a separate reliability problem from fluency [ts=4401s]. Another risk comes from pretraining data: internet corpora contain harmful stereotypes and biases that models can repeat when prompted, while fully cleaning web-scale data is extremely costly [ts=4457s] [ts=4480s]. Finally, the lecture discusses adversarial triggers and toxic degeneration, concluding that models should not be deployed without safeguards, trigger-word robustness checks, and careful thought about user interaction [ts=4496s] [ts=4571s]. The closing outlook says that despite ChatGPT's strengths, major limitations remain in creativity, deep reasoning, and especially reliable evaluation, leaving NLG as an active and exciting research area [ts=4612s] [ts=4638s].

key_points.text: NLG systems should align with human values by avoiding harmful content, toxicity, and unfair bias across demographic groups.
Jailbreak prompts can bypass safety behavior even when a model usually refuses toxic requests.
Large language models can be confidently untruthful, producing factual errors in persuasive language.
Internet-scale pretraining data can encode harmful stereotypes, and full data cleaning is labor-intensive and nearly infeasible.
Universal adversarial triggers and even innocuous prompts can elicit toxic content from pretrained models.
Before deploying or publishing NLG systems, check that outputs are not harmful and that the model is robust to trigger words and adversarial prompts.

concepts_kp_local.name: Natural language generation
concepts_kp_local.description: The family of NLP tasks whose output is natural language intended to be fluent, coherent, and useful for people.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It defines the problem family that organizes every later discussion of models, decoding, training, evaluation, and risks.

concepts_kp_local.name: Open-endedness and entropy in NLG tasks
concepts_kp_local.description: A way to categorize generation tasks by how many valid outputs they permit, with entropy used as a formal intuition for openness.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The lecture repeatedly uses task openness to motivate different decoding, architecture, and evaluation choices.

concepts_kp_local.name: Autoregressive language modeling
concepts_kp_local.description: Left-to-right generation where each next token is predicted from previously generated context and fed back into the model.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It is the core generation mechanism underlying both the decoding and training discussions in the rest of the lecture.

concepts_kp_local.name: Teacher forcing and student forcing
concepts_kp_local.description: The contrast between training with gold previous tokens and generating with the model's own previous outputs.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: This distinction is necessary for understanding exposure bias and why generation-time behavior diverges from training-time assumptions.

concepts_kp_local.name: Degeneration under maximum-probability decoding
concepts_kp_local.description: The failure mode where greedy or beam-style search produces repetitive, overconfident text in open-ended generation.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It motivates why open-ended generation needs different decoding strategies than low-entropy tasks.

concepts_kp_local.name: Sampling-based decoding controls
concepts_kp_local.description: The family of stochastic decoding methods and hyperparameters such as top-k, top-p, epsilon, typical sampling, and temperature.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: These methods are the main practical answer the lecture gives for generating diverse yet plausible open-ended text.

concepts_kp_local.name: Reranking sampled candidates
concepts_kp_local.description: Selecting among multiple sampled generations using auxiliary quality scores such as style, coherence, or factuality.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It is a practical refinement layer that improves generation quality without retraining the base language model.

concepts_kp_local.name: Exposure bias
concepts_kp_local.description: The train-test mismatch caused by training on gold prefixes but generating on self-produced prefixes.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It is the lecture's main training-side explanation for coherence loss and repetition in generated text.

concepts_kp_local.name: Training alternatives to reduce exposure bias
concepts_kp_local.description: Methods such as scheduled sampling, DAgger, and retrieval-augmented generation that make training conditions closer to generation conditions.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: These techniques are the lecture's concrete responses to exposure bias and train-test mismatch.

concepts_kp_local.name: Reinforcement learning and RLHF for NLG
concepts_kp_local.description: Training text generators with reward functions, including human-preference reward models layered on top of pretrained systems.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It integrates generation, reward design, and alignment, and connects the lecture to modern ChatGPT-style pipelines.

concepts_kp_local.name: Automatic evaluation metrics for NLG
concepts_kp_local.description: The landscape of reference-based lexical and embedding-based automatic metrics used to score generated text.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Evaluation quality determines whether model improvements are real, so understanding metric failure modes is central to the lecture.

concepts_kp_local.name: Human-centered evaluation and metric alignment
concepts_kp_local.description: Using human judgments as the gold standard and measuring whether automatic metrics agree with those judgments.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The lecture treats correlation with human judgment as the gold criterion for judging evaluation methods.

concepts_kp_local.name: Ethical risks and deployment safeguards for NLG
concepts_kp_local.description: The safety, bias, truthfulness, and adversarial robustness considerations that should gate deployment of text generators.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The lecture ends by stressing that useful NLG systems must also be non-harmful, robust, and responsibly deployed.

unit_kp_map_local.coverage_rationale: The segment explicitly defines NLG and anchors it with multiple examples including MT, dialogue, summarization, and ChatGPT [ts=155s] [ts=214s] [ts=280s].
The lecture develops the open-endedness spectrum and formalizes it with entropy as the main organizing axis for NLG tasks [ts=348s] [ts=461s] [ts=493s].
Autoregressive next-token generation is explained step by step, including score computation, softmax, token selection, and feedback into the model [ts=528s] [ts=742s].
Teacher forcing and student forcing are contrasted directly, setting up the later exposure-bias discussion [ts=672s] [ts=858s].
Architecture preference is motivated by whether a task is more or less open-ended [ts=577s].
The segment centers on how greedy and beam decoding cause repetition and overconfident degeneration in open-ended generation [ts=968s] [ts=1039s] [ts=1232s].
Decoding is framed as selecting from the autoregressive model's next-token distribution [ts=915s].
Sampling, top-k, top-p, epsilon, typical sampling, and temperature are the main focus of the segment [ts=1356s] [ts=1456s] [ts=1741s] [ts=1875s] [ts=2009s].
The latter part of the segment explains generating multiple samples and selecting the best one with auxiliary scoring functions [ts=2150s] [ts=2218s].
Sampling is motivated as a response to the earlier failure of maximum-probability decoding and repetition [ts=1351s].
Exposure bias is explicitly defined as the key training-time mismatch behind bad generation behavior [ts=2510s] [ts=2541s].
Scheduled sampling, DAgger, and retrieval-plus-generation are introduced as remedies that make training and test conditions closer [ts=2560s] [ts=2603s] [ts=2655s].
RL and RLHF are presented as reward-based training extensions, including the modern pretraining -> instruction tuning -> RLHF pipeline [ts=2693s] [ts=2827s] [ts=3110s].
The segment surveys BLEU, ROUGE, embedding similarity, word mover's distance, BERTScore, and BLEURT as automatic evaluation metrics [ts=3310s] [ts=3582s] [ts=3627s] [ts=3678s].
MAUVE is introduced as another automatic metric, now specialized to open-ended generation [ts=3738s].
The segment makes human judgment the gold standard for evaluating metrics and models, then details human-study criteria and limitations [ts=4048s] [ts=4115s] [ts=4153s].
The ethics section covers toxicity, bias, jailbreaks, factual errors, adversarial triggers, and explicit deployment checks [ts=4332s] [ts=4401s] [ts=4496s] [ts=4571s].
Alignment with human values is invoked again in the ethics discussion as a deployment objective for modern LLMs [ts=4332s].

section_flags.rationale: This is the conceptual framing section that defines NLG and introduces the task taxonomy the rest of the lecture relies on [ts=155s] [ts=493s].
Core mechanics of autoregressive modeling and teacher forcing are introduced here and reused in both decoding and training sections [ts=528s] [ts=672s].
This segment motivates the entire decoding toolbox by diagnosing degeneration and repetition under greedy and beam search [ts=968s] [ts=1039s].
The segment contains the densest practical decoding content, including top-k, top-p, temperature, and reranking [ts=1456s] [ts=1741s] [ts=2009s] [ts=2150s].
Exposure bias and its remedies are central training concepts, and the section also links them to RLHF-style modern systems [ts=2510s] [ts=2560s] [ts=3110s].
These automatic metrics are likely to be tested or reused in projects because the lecture emphasizes their strengths and failure modes [ts=3310s] [ts=3459s] [ts=3582s].
The segment gives the lecture's most explicit guidance on what makes an evaluation method trustworthy and why human judgment remains the gold standard [ts=4048s] [ts=4115s] [ts=4153s].
This section is important for deployment judgment and responsible use, focusing on toxicity, bias, truthfulness, and adversarial robustness [ts=4332s] [ts=4401s] [ts=4571s].
